bytes.topic: Framework Choice — PyTorch vs TensorFlow vs JAX, when each makes sense
bytes.title: PyTorch vs TensorFlow vs JAX — Picking the Right Framework for Your ML Workflow
bytes.body:
PyTorch's define-by-run (eager) execution makes it the default for research: you get Python-native control flow, real-time tensor inspection with `.item()` / `print(tensor)`, and painless custom autograd via `torch.autograd.Function`. The imperative style means debugging is just plain Python debugging.

Where PyTorch loses ground: production serving at scale. TensorFlow's SavedModel + TF Serving pipeline is more mature for stateless inference behind load balancers, and TFLite/TF.js cover edge/browser targets PyTorch only partially addresses via ONNX export.

JAX wins when you need functional purity and hardware-level control: `jax.jit` + `jax.vmap` + `jax.pmap` compose cleanly for batched, parallelized, XLA-compiled kernels. JAX shines for custom optimizers, second-order gradients (`jax.hessian`), and TPU-first research. PyTorch's equivalent (`torch.compile` + `torch.vmap`) is catching up but still lags in composability.

Default to PyTorch for prototyping and most deep learning research. Switch to TensorFlow if your org's serving infra is TF-centric. Reach for JAX when you need differentiable everything or TPU performance at the algorithm level.

bytes.topic: Tensor Operations — fundamentals, broadcasting, device management (CPU/GPU)
bytes.title: PyTorch Tensor Operations — Broadcasting, Device Management, and What Actually Runs on GPU
bytes.body:
PyTorch tensors are the core compute unit, and three things trip up daily users: broadcasting semantics, in-place vs out-of-place ops, and silent CPU/GPU mismatches.

Broadcasting follows NumPy rules but with autograd implications — broadcasting during a backward pass allocates extra memory to sum gradients across broadcast dimensions. Know this when you have large batch dimensions.

Device management: tensors created with `torch.tensor()` land on CPU by default. Use `torch.zeros(..., device='cuda')` or `.to(device)` to move them. Mixing devices raises a RuntimeError, but the error only surfaces at op time, not at assignment — so a model weight on GPU and an input on CPU will fail silently until forward(). Pin memory with `pin_memory=True` in DataLoader to accelerate host-to-device transfers via DMA.

In-place ops (trailing `_` like `add_()`, `fill_()`) mutate the tensor and can corrupt the autograd graph if the tensor is part of a computation graph. Use them only on leaf tensors with `requires_grad=False`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')

# Explicit device placement at creation
x = torch.ones(3, 1, device=device)
y = torch.ones(1, 4, device=device)

# Broadcasting: (3,1) + (1,4) -> (3,4) without copies
z = x + y
print(z.shape)  # torch.Size([3, 4])

# Safe in-place op: leaf tensor, no grad
buffer = torch.zeros(3, 4, device=device)
buffer.add_(z)  # OK — no grad tracked

# Move existing tensor; non-blocking avoids stalling CPU
t = torch.randn(1000, 1000)
t = t.to(device, non_blocking=True)

bytes.topic: Model Architecture — layers, activation functions, building custom modules
bytes.title: PyTorch Custom Modules — Building Reusable Layers with nn.Module
bytes.body:
Every learnable component in PyTorch inherits from `nn.Module`. When you subclass it, you get parameter tracking, `.to(device)`, `.train()`/`.eval()` toggling, and `state_dict()` serialization for free. Define learnable weights in `__init__` using `nn.Parameter` or registered submodules — never as plain tensors, or they'll be invisible to the optimizer. The `forward()` method describes the computation graph; backprop is handled automatically via autograd.

For activation functions, prefer `F.gelu` or `F.silu` over `F.relu` in modern transformer-style architectures — they're smooth and empirically stronger. Use `nn.Sequential` for linear stacks, but reach for a custom module the moment you need residual connections, gating, or conditional logic.

A practical pattern: wrap your normalization + activation + dropout into a single `Block` module. This keeps `forward()` readable, lets you swap internals cleanly, and makes the block reusable across encoder/decoder stacks. Call `self.apply(init_weights)` in `__init__` to initialize all child modules recursively without touching each layer manually.

bytes.topic: Training Loop — forward pass, loss computation, backpropagation, optimizer step
bytes.title: PyTorch Training Loop — Forward Pass to Optimizer Step
bytes.body:
Every PyTorch training iteration follows a strict four-step rhythm. First, the forward pass: call `model(inputs)` to compute predictions — PyTorch builds a dynamic computation graph on-the-fly via autograd. Second, compute loss using a criterion like `nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(outputs, targets)`. Third, backpropagation: call `loss.backward()`, which traverses the computation graph in reverse and accumulates gradients into each parameter's `.grad` attribute. Fourth, `optimizer.step()` applies those gradients to update weights using the rule defined by your optimizer (SGD, Adam, etc.).

Critically, `optimizer.zero_grad()` must be called before `loss.backward()` — PyTorch accumulates gradients by default, so skipping this causes gradients from previous batches to bleed into the current update. Set `set_to_none=True` for a small memory win. For mixed-precision training, wrap the forward pass in `torch.autocast` and replace `loss.backward()` + `optimizer.step()` with `scaler.scale(loss).backward()` + `scaler.step(optimizer)` using a `GradScaler`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model.train()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for inputs, targets in dataloader:
    inputs, targets = inputs.to(device), targets.to(device)

    optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)  # clear stale gradients
    outputs = model(inputs)                # forward pass + graph build
    loss = criterion(outputs, targets)     # scalar loss tensor
    loss.backward()                        # backprop: populate .grad
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    optimizer.step()                       # weight update

bytes.topic: Optimizers — SGD, Adam, AdamW, learning rate schedulers
bytes.title: PyTorch Optimizers — SGD, Adam, AdamW, and LR Schedulers Explained
bytes.body: PyTorch ships all major optimizers in `torch.optim`. `SGD` with `momentum=0.9` and `weight_decay` is still competitive for vision tasks but sensitive to learning rate. `Adam` uses adaptive per-parameter learning rates via first and second moment estimates (`betas=(0.9, 0.999)`), making it forgiving — but its L2 regularization via `weight_decay` is broken: it decouples incorrectly because decay is applied to the gradient, not the weight. `AdamW` fixes this by applying weight decay directly to parameters, decoupled from the gradient update — prefer it over `Adam` for transformers and fine-tuning. Pair any optimizer with a scheduler. `CosineAnnealingLR` is the default choice for most training runs. `OneCycleLR` enables super-convergence in fewer epochs. Always call `scheduler.step()` after `optimizer.step()`. Use `torch.optim.lr_scheduler.get_last_lr()` to log the current LR. For mixed-precision training with `torch.amp`, gradient scaling with `GradScaler` must wrap the backward pass before `optimizer.step()`.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingLR

model = nn.Linear(128, 10)
optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=0.01, betas=(0.9, 0.999))
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=100, eta_min=1e-6)

for epoch in range(100):
    loss = compute_loss(model)  # your training step
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch} | LR: {scheduler.get_last_lr()[0]:.2e}")

bytes.topic: Loss Functions — choosing the right loss, custom loss functions
bytes.title: PyTorch Loss Functions — Choosing Right and Writing Custom Ones
bytes.body:
PyTorch ships with 20+ loss functions in `torch.nn`, but the wrong choice tanks training silently. For binary classification, `BCEWithLogitsLoss` beats `BCELoss` — it fuses sigmoid + BCE in a single numerically stable op, avoiding log(0) explosions. For multi-class, `CrossEntropyLoss` already applies `log_softmax` internally; wrapping it in an explicit `Softmax` layer double-applies it and destroys gradients.

Custom losses are just `torch.autograd`-compatible functions — subclass `nn.Module` or use a plain function. The key constraint: every operation must stay in the PyTorch computation graph (no NumPy detours mid-forward). For imbalanced datasets, a custom Focal Loss adjusts per-sample weight by prediction confidence, penalizing easy negatives less. Use `reduction='none'` on a base loss to get per-element tensors, apply your weighting, then `.mean()`. Register no extra parameters unless your loss has learnable weights (e.g., uncertainty weighting in multi-task learning via `nn.Parameter`).
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class FocalLoss(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, gamma: float = 2.0, alpha: float = 0.25):
        super().__init__()
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.alpha = alpha

    def forward(self, logits: torch.Tensor, targets: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        bce = F.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits, targets, reduction='none')
        pt = torch.exp(-bce)  # probability of correct class
        focal_weight = self.alpha * (1 - pt) ** self.gamma
        return (focal_weight * bce).mean()

# Usage
criterion = FocalLoss(gamma=2.0, alpha=0.25)
loss = criterion(logits, targets.float())

bytes.topic: Data Loading — DataLoader, batching, shuffling, prefetching, augmentation
bytes.title: PyTorch DataLoader — Batching, Shuffling, Prefetching, and Augmentation Done Right
bytes.body:
PyTorch's `DataLoader` wraps any `Dataset` and handles the heavy lifting of batching, shuffling, and parallel data loading. Set `num_workers > 0` to spawn worker processes that prefetch batches while the GPU trains — on Linux, `num_workers=4` is a safe default; on Windows, wrap your script in `if __name__ == '__main__'` to avoid multiprocessing errors. `pin_memory=True` allocates batches in page-locked memory, making `.to(device)` transfers faster when using CUDA. Enable `persistent_workers=True` to keep worker processes alive across epochs, cutting per-epoch startup overhead.

For augmentation, apply transforms inside `__getitem__` using `torchvision.transforms.v2` (the updated API that handles bounding boxes and masks). Avoid heavy transforms in the main process — workers parallelize them for free. Use `prefetch_factor` (default 2) to control how many batches each worker prepopulates. For variable-length sequences, pass a custom `collate_fn` to `DataLoader` instead of relying on the default, which will error on ragged tensors. Combine `drop_last=True` with `shuffle=True` to prevent a partial final batch from skewing batch norm statistics.
bytes.code_snippet:
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
from torchvision.transforms import v2

class MyDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, data, transforms=None):
        self.data = data
        self.transforms = transforms

    def __len__(self): return len(self.data)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        img, label = self.data[idx]
        if self.transforms:
            img = self.transforms(img)
        return img, label

transforms = v2.Compose([v2.RandomHorizontalFlip(), v2.ToDtype(torch.float32, scale=True)])
dataset = MyDataset(raw_data, transforms=transforms)

loader = DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=64,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=4,
    pin_memory=True,
    persistent_workers=True,
    drop_last=True,
)

bytes.topic: Regularization — dropout, weight decay, batch normalization, early stopping
bytes.title: PyTorch Regularization — Dropout, Weight Decay, BatchNorm, and Early Stopping in Practice
bytes.body: PyTorch gives you four complementary regularization tools, each targeting a different failure mode. `nn.Dropout(p=0.5)` randomly zeroes activations during training — call `model.train()` and `model.eval()` to toggle it correctly, or it'll silently apply at inference. Weight decay is passed directly to the optimizer: `torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)` — prefer `AdamW` over `Adam + L2` since Adam's adaptive scaling makes raw L2 ineffective. `nn.BatchNorm1d` / `nn.BatchNorm2d` normalizes per-batch activations and carries its own learnable `weight` and `bias` parameters; it also behaves differently under `train()` vs `eval()` (running stats vs batch stats). Early stopping isn't built-in — track validation loss manually and checkpoint with `torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'best.pt')`, restoring via `model.load_state_dict(torch.load('best.pt'))` when patience expires. Combine all four: BatchNorm before activation, Dropout after, AdamW with decay, early stopping on val loss.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class RegularizedNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(128, 64),
            nn.BatchNorm1d(64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Dropout(p=0.4),
            nn.Linear(64, 10)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

model = RegularizedNet()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)

best_val_loss = float('inf')
patience, strikes = 5, 0

for epoch in range(100):
    model.train()
    # ... training loop ...

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_loss = compute_val_loss()  # your validation logic

    if val_loss < best_val_loss:
        best_val_loss = val_loss
        strikes = 0
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), 'best.pt')
    else:
        strikes += 1
        if strikes >= patience:
            model.load_state_dict(torch.load('best.pt'))
            break

bytes.topic: Transfer Learning — pretrained models, fine-tuning vs feature extraction, frozen layers
bytes.title: PyTorch Transfer Learning — Fine-Tuning vs Feature Extraction with Pretrained Models
bytes.body:
PyTorch's `torchvision.models` gives you ImageNet-pretrained backbones in one line. The key decision: freeze the backbone and train only the head (feature extraction), or unfreeze some layers and train end-to-end (fine-tuning).

For feature extraction, set `requires_grad = False` on all parameters before replacing the classifier head. This is fast and prevents catastrophic forgetting when your dataset is small.

For fine-tuning, unfreeze later layers — typically the last residual block in ResNets — and use a lower learning rate (1e-4 or less) for those layers versus a higher rate for the new head. PyTorch's optimizer accepts per-layer `param_groups` for exactly this.

The rule of thumb: small dataset + similar domain → feature extraction. Large dataset or domain shift → fine-tune deeper layers.

Always call `model.train()` and `model.eval()` correctly — frozen BatchNorm layers still need `eval()` mode to use running stats rather than batch stats during inference, or you'll get noisy predictions.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torchvision.models as models
import torch.nn as nn

model = models.resnet50(weights=models.ResNet50_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V2)

# Freeze all layers
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

# Replace head for 10-class problem
model.fc = nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 10)

# Fine-tune: unfreeze layer4 with a lower lr
for param in model.layer4.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = True

optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([
    {"params": model.layer4.parameters(), "lr": 1e-4},
    {"params": model.fc.parameters(),     "lr": 1e-3},
])

bytes.topic: GPU Acceleration — CUDA, moving tensors to device, mixed precision training
bytes.title: PyTorch GPU Acceleration — CUDA Devices, Tensor Movement, and Mixed Precision
bytes.body:
PyTorch's device abstraction makes GPU acceleration explicit and intentional. Every tensor lives on a specific device — CPU by default — and cross-device operations raise errors, not silent slowdowns.

Use `torch.device('cuda')` or the shorthand `'cuda:0'` to target a specific GPU. Move tensors with `.to(device)` or `.cuda()`, and always move your model before your optimizer is constructed — optimizer state inherits the model's device at init time.

For mixed precision training, `torch.cuda.amp` provides `autocast` and `GradScaler`. Wrapping your forward pass in `autocast()` lets PyTorch automatically cast eligible ops to `float16`, cutting memory usage roughly in half and boosting throughput on Ampere+ GPUs. `GradScaler` compensates for `float16`'s limited dynamic range by scaling loss before backward and unscaling before the optimizer step, skipping updates when inf/nan gradients appear.

Always call `scaler.update()` at the end of each step — skipping it causes the scale factor to stagnate. Profile with `torch.cuda.synchronize()` before timing to avoid measuring async launches.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
from torch.cuda.amp import autocast, GradScaler

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
model = MyModel().to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)
scaler = GradScaler()

for inputs, labels in dataloader:
    inputs, labels = inputs.to(device), labels.to(device)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    with autocast():
        outputs = model(inputs)
        loss = criterion(outputs, labels)
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()

bytes.topic: Debugging Models — gradient flow, NaN loss, exploding/vanishing gradients
bytes.title: PyTorch Debugging — Gradient Flow, NaN Loss, and Exploding/Vanishing Gradients
bytes.body:
When loss becomes NaN, start with `torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly(True)` — it pinpoints the exact operation that produced the NaN, at a ~2x speed cost. Remove it in production.

For vanishing gradients, register backward hooks to inspect gradient magnitudes per layer. A common pattern is iterating `model.named_parameters()` after `loss.backward()` and checking `.grad.norm()`. Gradients consistently below 1e-6 in early layers signal vanishing. For exploding gradients, `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)` is the standard fix — call it before `optimizer.step()`.

To visualize gradient flow across all layers, hook into each module's `.register_full_backward_hook()` or plot `p.grad.abs().mean()` per named parameter. Dead ReLU neurons show zero gradients entirely — switch to `nn.LeakyReLU` or check your weight initialization with `nn.init.kaiming_uniform_`.

For NaN loss from mixed precision, wrap suspect ops with `torch.autocast` carefully and use `GradScaler.unscale_()` before clipping to inspect raw gradients before scaling distorts them.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def check_gradient_flow(model):
    for name, param in model.named_parameters():
        if param.grad is not None:
            grad_norm = param.grad.norm().item()
            print(f"{name}: grad_norm={grad_norm:.6f}")
            if torch.isnan(param.grad).any():
                print(f"  ^^^ NaN gradient detected!")

# During training loop:
torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly(True)  # remove in prod
loss.backward()
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
check_gradient_flow(model)
optimizer.step()

bytes.topic: Model Evaluation — metrics per task type, confusion matrix, cross-validation
bytes.title: PyTorch Model Evaluation — Metrics, Confusion Matrix, and Cross-Validation
bytes.body:
Task type dictates your metric choice. For classification, use `torchmetrics.Accuracy`, `F1Score`, and `ConfusionMatrix` — not manual loops. For regression, `MeanSquaredError` and `MeanAbsoluteError` from the same library. Always move tensors to CPU before numpy ops: `preds.cpu().numpy()`.

The `torchmetrics` library handles device placement and batched accumulation automatically. Instantiate metrics once, call `.update(preds, targets)` per batch, then `.compute()` at epoch end — never accumulate raw tensors manually.

For confusion matrices on multi-class problems, set `task='multiclass'` and `num_classes=N`. Visualize with `seaborn.heatmap(cm.numpy())`.

Cross-validation in PyTorch requires manual fold management — use `sklearn.model_selection.KFold` to generate indices, then re-initialize your `nn.Module` and optimizer each fold. There's no built-in CV loop. Reset metric state between folds with `.reset()`. Track per-fold scores in a plain list, then `torch.tensor(scores).mean()` for the final estimate.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torchmetrics
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

metric = torchmetrics.classification.MulticlassF1Score(num_classes=5, average='macro').to(device)
cm_metric = torchmetrics.classification.MulticlassConfusionMatrix(num_classes=5).to(device)

for preds, targets in val_loader:
    preds, targets = preds.to(device), targets.to(device)
    metric.update(preds, targets)
    cm_metric.update(preds, targets)

f1 = metric.compute()          # tensor(0.87)
cm = cm_metric.compute()       # shape: [5, 5]
metric.reset()
cm_metric.reset()

bytes.topic: scikit-learn — pipeline API, preprocessing, cross-validation, model selection
bytes.title: PyTorch + scikit-learn Pipeline — Grid Search Your nn.Module with skorch
bytes.body:
PyTorch's training loop gives full control, but wiring up cross-validation and hyperparameter search by hand is tedious. `skorch` bridges this gap by wrapping any `nn.Module` in a scikit-learn-compatible estimator — enabling sklearn's full `Pipeline`, `GridSearchCV`, and `cross_val_score` machinery on PyTorch models.

`NeuralNetClassifier` accepts your module class, not an instance — skorch instantiates it per fold with the right `module__*` kwargs. This means you can parameterize architecture choices (hidden size, dropout rate) directly in the param grid alongside optimizer settings like `lr` and `optimizer__weight_decay`.

Plugging the wrapped net into a `Pipeline` keeps preprocessing (e.g., `StandardScaler`) tightly coupled to the model, preventing data leakage across CV folds — a common bug when scaling outside the pipeline. `GridSearchCV` handles k-fold splitting, refitting on the best params, and exposes `best_params_`, `cv_results_`, and the final estimator.

For large search spaces, swap `GridSearchCV` for `RandomizedSearchCV` and pass `scipy.stats` distributions as param ranges — identical API, far fewer fits.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch.nn as nn
from skorch import NeuralNetClassifier
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

class Net(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(20, hidden), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(hidden, 2))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.fc(x)

net = NeuralNetClassifier(Net, max_epochs=10, lr=0.01, verbose=0)
pipe = Pipeline([("scale", StandardScaler()), ("net", net)])
params = {"net__module__hidden": [32, 64], "net__lr": [0.01, 0.001]}
gs = GridSearchCV(pipe, params, cv=3, scoring="accuracy")
gs.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(gs.best_params_)

bytes.topic: XGBoost — gradient boosting, hyperparameter tuning, feature importance
bytes.title: PyTorch + XGBoost — Gradient Boosting on Frozen Encoder Embeddings
bytes.body:
When your PyTorch encoder produces high-dimensional embeddings (256–2048-dim), XGBoost often outperforms a fully-connected head on small-to-medium tabular targets. The pattern: freeze the encoder, extract features, hand off to gradient boosting for the final decision boundary.

Extract with `model.eval()` + `torch.no_grad()`, convert via `.detach().cpu().numpy()` — XGBoost's `DMatrix` takes the raw array directly. Set `"device": "cuda"` in params to keep compute on the same GPU your PyTorch model occupies, eliminating host-device round trips.

For hyperparameter tuning, `xgb.cv()` with `early_stopping_rounds` beats manual grid search — it finds optimal `num_boost_round` without overfitting. Key params on dense embeddings: `max_depth` (3–6), `eta` (0.01–0.1), `subsample` + `colsample_bytree` (0.6–0.9 prevents over-reliance on correlated embedding dims).

Feature importance via `get_score(importance_type="gain")` reveals which embedding dimensions drive predictions — a practical diagnostic for encoder collapse or redundancy in contrastive learning setups. This hybrid is especially strong for transfer learning on small labeled datasets where a trainable MLP head would overfit.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import xgboost as xgb

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    feats = model.encoder(x_tensor.to(device)).detach().cpu().numpy()

dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(feats, label=y_np)
params = {
    "objective": "binary:logistic",
    "max_depth": 4,
    "eta": 0.05,
    "subsample": 0.8,
    "colsample_bytree": 0.8,
    "device": "cuda",
    "eval_metric": "auc",
}
cv = xgb.cv(params, dtrain, num_boost_round=300, nfold=5, early_stopping_rounds=15)
best_round = int(cv["test-auc-mean"].idxmax())
bst = xgb.train(params, dtrain, num_boost_round=best_round)
print(bst.get_score(importance_type="gain"))

bytes.topic: JAX — functional transformations (jit, grad, vmap), XLA compilation
bytes.title: PyTorch vs JAX — jit, grad, and vmap as Functional Transforms
bytes.body:
JAX rewires PyTorch's imperative mental model into pure functional transforms. Where PyTorch uses tape-based autograd — `loss.backward()` mutates `.grad` in-place — JAX's `jax.grad` returns a new function that computes gradients directly. No retained computation graph, no side effects.

`jax.jit` traces your function to XLA HLO bytecode, compiling once for the target accelerator. Unlike `torch.compile` (TorchInductor/Triton), XLA performs aggressive cross-operation fusion at the HLO level — larger fused kernels, but a cold-start penalty on first call. Dynamic shapes break JIT caching; pin critical dims with `static_argnums`.

`jax.vmap` auto-vectorizes a single-example function across a batch axis — directly analogous to `torch.vmap` from `torch.func`. The constraint: your function must be pure. PyTorch modules carry state (`nn.Module` parameters), so batching them requires `torch.func.functional_call` to extract params as plain tensors first.

The payoff is composability: `jax.jit(jax.vmap(jax.grad(loss_fn)))` stacks cleanly. PyTorch achieves functional parity via `torch.func`, but the style remains non-idiomatic for most PyTorch codebases.
bytes.code_snippet:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp

# Pure function — no in-place ops (unlike PyTorch nn.Module forward)
def loss_fn(params, x, y):
    return jnp.mean((x @ params - y) ** 2)

# grad: returns gradient fn (cf. loss.backward() + param.grad in PyTorch)
grad_fn = jax.grad(loss_fn)  # differentiates w.r.t. first arg (params)

# jit: XLA-compiles to fused HLO kernels (cf. torch.compile)
fast_grad = jax.jit(grad_fn)

# vmap: vectorizes over batch axis (cf. torch.vmap in torch.func)
# Compose all three — idiomatic JAX, non-idiomatic in vanilla PyTorch
compiled_batched_grad = jax.jit(jax.vmap(jax.grad(loss_fn), in_axes=(None, 0, 0)))

bytes.topic: Keras — high-level API, functional vs sequential, custom layers and training loops
bytes.title: PyTorch + Keras 3 — Sequential, Functional, and Custom Layers on a Torch Backend
bytes.body:
Keras 3 is backend-agnostic — set `KERAS_BACKEND=torch` before importing Keras and every tensor is a `torch.Tensor`. Sequential API stacks layers linearly; the functional API wires arbitrary DAGs via `keras.Input`, enabling multi-input/output graphs native PyTorch `nn.Sequential` can't express without boilerplate.

For custom ops, subclass `keras.Layer` and implement `call(self, x, training=None)`. Keras automatically passes `training=True` during `model.fit()`, so dropout and batch norm behave correctly without manually toggling `model.train()` / `model.eval()`.

Custom training loops stay pure PyTorch: iterate your `DataLoader`, call `model(x, training=True)`, compute loss as a `torch.Tensor`, then call `.backward()` + `optimizer.step()` normally. Model weights are accessible via both `model.trainable_weights` (Keras Variables) and `list(model.parameters())` — Keras registers them as `torch.nn.Parameter` under the hood.

Pass a native `torch.optim.Adam` to `model.compile(optimizer=...)` for full PyTorch optimizer compatibility including schedulers and `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_`. This lets you prototype with Keras ergonomics while retaining `torch.compile`, custom CUDA kernels, and Hugging Face integrations.
bytes.code_snippet:
import os
os.environ["KERAS_BACKEND"] = "torch"
import keras, torch

# Functional API — multi-branch graph
inputs = keras.Input(shape=(128,))
x = keras.layers.Dense(64, activation="relu")(inputs)
outputs = keras.layers.Dense(10)(x)
model = keras.Model(inputs, outputs)

# Custom layer — receives/returns torch.Tensor
class L2Dense(keras.Layer):
    def __init__(self, units):
        super().__init__()
        self.dense = keras.layers.Dense(units)
    def call(self, x, training=None):
        out = self.dense(x, training=training)
        return out / (torch.norm(out, dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-6)

# Native PyTorch training loop
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)
for x_batch, y_batch in dataloader:
    loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(model(x_batch, training=True), y_batch)
    opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()

bytes.topic: Saving & Loading — checkpointing, model serialization, format choices
bytes.title: PyTorch Checkpointing — torch.save, state_dict, and When to Use SafeTensors
bytes.body:
PyTorch gives you two main serialization paths: pickling entire model objects with torch.save(model, path) or saving just the weights with torch.save(model.state_dict(), path). Always prefer state_dict. Pickling the full model couples the saved file to your exact class definition — rename a module, move a file, and loading breaks silently or raises cryptic errors.

For training checkpoints, save more than weights: include optimizer state, scheduler state, epoch, and loss so training can resume exactly. torch.load uses Python's pickle by default, which means arbitrary code execution on untrusted files — a real risk when sharing weights publicly.

For inference and distribution, prefer the SafeTensors format (pip install safetensors). It stores raw tensor data with no pickle, loads faster via memory-mapping, and is safe to open from untrusted sources. HuggingFace models ship in this format by default now.

For large models, torch.save with map_location='cpu' on load prevents CUDA OOM when loading on a different device configuration than you trained on.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
from safetensors.torch import save_file, load_file

# Save a resumable training checkpoint
checkpoint = {
    'epoch': epoch,
    'model_state_dict': model.state_dict(),
    'optimizer_state_dict': optimizer.state_dict(),
    'loss': loss,
}
torch.save(checkpoint, 'checkpoint.pt')

# Resume training
checkpoint = torch.load('checkpoint.pt', map_location='cpu')
model.load_state_dict(checkpoint['model_state_dict'])
optimizer.load_state_dict(checkpoint['optimizer_state_dict'])

# Safer inference weights — no pickle
save_file(model.state_dict(), 'model.safetensors')
state_dict = load_file('model.safetensors', device='cuda')
model.load_state_dict(state_dict)

bytes.topic: Production Readiness — model export, ONNX conversion, serving considerations
bytes.title: PyTorch Production Readiness — ONNX Export, TorchScript, and Serving
bytes.body: Shipping a PyTorch model to production means choosing between three export paths: TorchScript, ONNX, or torch.export (the new dynamo-based path in 2.x). TorchScript via torch.jit.trace works well for static graphs but silently ignores Python control flow — use torch.jit.script for models with branches. For cross-runtime serving (ONNX Runtime, TensorRT, CoreML), torch.onnx.export is the standard, but watch for opset mismatches: most production runtimes target opset 17 or 18. Always pass dynamic_axes for variable batch/sequence sizes or your model will be hardcoded to the traced input shape. Post-export, validate outputs with numpy allclose against the original model — quantization and op fusion can introduce float drift. For high-throughput REST serving, torchserve handles model versioning, batching, and metrics out of the box. If latency is critical, compile with torch.compile(model, backend='onnxrt') to fuse ops before export. Never serve a raw .pth weights file — it requires the original class definition at load time, creating a fragile coupling.
bytes.code_snippet:
import torch
import torch.onnx
import numpy as np
import onnxruntime as ort

model = MyModel().eval()
dummy = torch.randn(1, 3, 224, 224)

torch.onnx.export(
    model, dummy, "model.onnx",
    opset_version=18,
    input_names=["input"],
    output_names=["output"],
    dynamic_axes={"input": {0: "batch_size"}, "output": {0: "batch_size"}}
)

session = ort.InferenceSession("model.onnx", providers=["CUDAExecutionProvider"])
onnx_out = session.run(None, {"input": dummy.numpy()})[0]

with torch.no_grad():
    torch_out = model(dummy).numpy()

assert np.allclose(torch_out, onnx_out, atol=1e-5), "Output mismatch — check opset or quantization"
